sub: How to communicate effectively with AI and manage its limitations

title: Prompt Engineering for Developers
desc: The most transferable daily skill in AI-assisted development.

items: 1. Explicit criteria > vague instructions
2. Few-shot examples — the single most powerful technique
3. Structured output — JSON schemas for guaranteed format
4. The interview pattern — let AI ask before implementing

body.quote: Good prompts are not about being polite or verbose. They are about removing ambiguity.

body.code: // BAD: vague "Check that the comments are accurate." // GOOD: explicit criteria "Flag comments ONLY when the claimed behavior contradicts the actual code behavior. Ignore style preferences and subjective opinions." // BAD: vague "Write good tests." // GOOD: explicit criteria "Write JUnit 5 tests using AAA pattern. Cover: happy path, null input, boundary values. Each test must have exactly one assertion."

body.quote: Specificity is not about length. It is about removing ways for the AI to misinterpret you.

title: Few-Shot Examples: Show, Don’t Tell
desc: 2–3 examples eliminate ambiguity better than paragraphs of instructions.

body.code: "Review this code. Format findings like these examples: Example 1: File: Calculator.java:15 Severity: ERROR Issue: Division by zero not handled Fix: Add zero-check before divide() Example 2: File: Student.java:42 Severity: WARN Issue: equals() without hashCode() Fix: Override hashCode() using same fields"
body.note: The model generalizes from examples to novel patterns. Show edge cases in your examples.

title: The Interview Pattern
desc: Let the AI ask questions before implementing — like a senior developer would.

body.code: // Instead of: "Implement a caching layer for the grade lookups." // Use the interview pattern: "I need a caching layer for grade lookups. Before implementing, ask me questions about: - Invalidation strategy - Expected load and data volume - Failure modes and fallback behavior - Consistency requirements"
body.note: Claude asks → you think through design → better architecture before any code is written.

body.quote: The interview pattern forces design thinking. You get better results AND better understanding.

title: Context Windows: The Invisible Constraint
desc: Every AI model has a maximum amount of text it can process at once.

body.code: Context window = everything the model can see System prompt + CLAUDE.md rules + conversation history + file contents read + tool outputs + your current request ________________________ = total tokens consumed When you exceed the limit: older context gets compressed or dropped
body.note: Claude Code’s context: 200K tokens. Sounds huge — but a large codebase fills it fast.

title: Lost-in-the-Middle Effect
desc: Models pay most attention to the beginning and end. The middle gets lost.

body.code: Attention ↑ | HIGH ... low attention zone ... HIGH | |||| |||| | |||| .... .... .... .... .... |||| | |||| .... .... .... .... .... |||| +--++++----++++--++++--++++--++++--++++----++++-→ Start End of context of context
body.note: Liu et al. (2023): “Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts”

items: Put important instructions at the start (system prompt, CLAUDE.md)
Put current task at the end (your latest message)
Long conversations = critical details drift to the middle and get missed

rows: Extract key findings into a persistent summary at the top
Don’t paste 500 lines of logs — extract the relevant 10
Write findings to a file that survives context compaction
Isolate verbose exploration in a subagent, return only summary
Compress conversation history, keeping essential facts
Continue work across sessions with preserved context

body.quote: Context management is not about saving tokens. It is about keeping the AI focused on what matters now.

title: Progressive Summarization Risks
desc: Every time context is compressed, specifics are lost.

body.code: Original fact: "The API rate limit is 429 requests/minute, measured per API key, reset at UTC midnight" After 1st compression: "API has a rate limit of ~400/min per key" After 2nd compression: "API has rate limiting" After 3rd compression: (fact is gone)
body.note: Numbers, dates, and specific thresholds are the first casualties of summarization.

items: Write critical facts to scratchpad files (persist outside context)
Use CLAUDE.md for facts that must never be lost
Repeat key constraints in your current message (end of context = high attention)

title: Human-in-the-Loop: When to Escalate
desc: Not every decision should be automated. Know when AI should stop and ask.

rows: AI proceeds autonomously (read files, run tests)
AI presents options with trade-offs, human chooses
AI stops and asks for clarification
Always requires human approval regardless of confidence

items: Structured errors: failure type + partial results + what was attempted
Never generic “failed” — agents downstream need to decide how to proceed
Coverage gaps must be explicitly reported, not silently ignored

title: cognitive-core: Team-Aware Estimation
desc: Real project planning distinguishes who does what: human, AI, or both.

rows: Human review of all AI output
table.note: Total wall-clock (parallel): ~4 hours. The bottleneck is always human review — not AI generation.

body.quote: Generic “5 developer-days” estimates hide reality. Tag every task: who validates it? If no human reviews AI output, the estimate is fiction.

items: Prompt quality is the most transferable daily skill in AI-assisted development
Explicit criteria > vague instructions — specificity removes ambiguity
Few-shot examples are the single most powerful prompting technique
The interview pattern: let AI ask questions before implementing
Context windows are finite — manage them or lose critical information
Lost-in-the-middle: important info at start and end, not buried in middle
Scratchpad files and /compact protect against progressive summarization
Human-in-the-loop: irreversible actions always require human approval regardless of AI confidence
